preamble: The palatalized voiceless velar plosive [kʲ] can undergo a phonetic transformation to the palatalized voiceless alveolar plosive [tʲ] through a process of extreme fronting or coronalization. This shift typically occurs under the influence of a preceding or following front vowel or palatal glide, where the velar articulation moves forward to the alveolar region while retaining its palatalized quality. This type of shift is a specific instance of velar fronting under palatalization, where the point of articulation moves from the velum to the alveolar ridge.
phoneticEffects: Fronting, Coronalization, Palatalization (maintenance of secondary articulation)

examples.note: The initial /k/ in Latin CĒPULLA, when borrowed into Basque, was likely palatalized to [kʲ] before the front vowel /i/. In some Basque dialects, this [kʲ] further fronted to [tʲ], resulting in forms like 'tipula' alongside 'kipula' for 'onion'.

sources: Prendergast, H. D. V., & Walker, C. C. (2004). Cultivated vegetables of the world: a multilingual onomasticon. Brill.
Etymologie-occitane. (2011, July 30). Ceba. Retrieved from https://www.etymologie-occitane.fr/ceba
